title: Raising Taxes Debate
description: Should the government significantly raise income and corporate taxes to fund expanded public services? This map explores fiscal, economic, and social arguments.

data.label: Raise top income tax rate to 50% and corporate tax to 28%
data.notes: Primary fiscal policy proposal under debate

data.label: Higher taxes generate revenue to fund healthcare, education, and infrastructure
data.notes: Core mechanism for expanded public services
data.mechanism: Increased marginal rates broaden fiscal capacity for social investment

data.label: Nordic countries with 50%+ top rates maintain strong public services and high living standards
data.notes: Denmark, Sweden, and Norway as comparative cases
data.citation: OECD Revenue Statistics (2023). Tax-to-GDP ratios across member nations.

data.label: US top marginal rate was 91% in the 1950s during strong economic growth
data.notes: Historical precedent for high top rates
data.citation: Tax Policy Center. Historical Highest Marginal Income Tax Rates.

data.label: Reducing inequality and ensuring equal opportunity
data.notes: Social justice and fairness as guiding principle

data.label: The wealthiest 1% hold more wealth than the bottom 50% combined
data.notes: Wealth concentration data

data.label: Tax revenue will be spent efficiently on intended programs
data.notes: Assumes effective government allocation

data.label: Higher corporate taxes reduce business investment and slow job creation
data.notes: Supply-side economics argument
data.mechanism: Reduced after-tax returns lower incentive to invest in expansion and hiring

data.label: US 2017 corporate tax cut from 35% to 21% did not produce promised investment surge
data.notes: Post-TCJA analysis shows most savings went to buybacks
data.citation: Hanlon, Hoopes & Slemrod (2019). Tax Reform Made Me Do It.

data.label: Higher income taxes may incentivize tax avoidance and capital flight
data.notes: Behavioral response to tax rate increases

data.label: Progressive taxation funds social mobility programs that boost long-run growth
data.notes: Public investment in human capital argument
data.mechanism: Education and healthcare spending creates more productive workforce

data.label: Individual economic freedom and property rights
data.notes: Libertarian and free-market perspective

data.label: Effective tax rates for the wealthy are far below statutory rates due to loopholes
data.notes: Distinction between marginal and effective rates

data.label: Capital and high earners are sufficiently mobile to relocate based on tax rates
data.notes: Assumption underlying the capital flight argument

data.label: Stanford study found only 0.04% of top earners relocated after California tax hikes
data.notes: Millionaire migration is much lower than claimed
data.citation: Young et al. (2016). Millionaire Migration and Taxation of the Elite.

data.notes: Revenue generation supports the policy
Nordic example shows high taxes can coexist with prosperity
Historical US precedent for high rates during growth
Equality values align with progressive taxation
Wealth concentration motivates inequality concerns
Revenue benefits assume competent spending
Investment reduction argument against tax hikes
TCJA evidence weakens investment-reduction claim
Avoidance risk undermines policy effectiveness
Capital flight claim depends on mobility assumption
Low migration rates challenge mobility assumption
Long-run growth through human capital investment
Economic freedom values underpin investment concerns
Tension between equality and economic freedom
If effective rates are already low, statutory increases may just close loopholes
Low effective rates justify raising statutory rates
Extreme concentration justifies redistribution
Long-run growth benefits counter short-run investment concerns
